title: Landscaping Services

labor_share.method: QCEW 2024 private total annual wages at 561730 ($45,367,770,541) x benefits multiplier 1.4294 [ECEC private-industry total comp $46.60/hr / wages $32.60/hr = 1.4294 (March 2026 reference period)] / SUSB 2022 receipts at 561730 ($118,363,300,000). Vintage mismatch: 2024 wages over 2022 receipts, stated. Level: 6-digit. Harmonized to IPS basis ÷1.3407 (median of 65 dual-method codes).

occupations.title: First-Line Supervisors of Landscaping, Lawn Service, and Groundskeeping Workers
Sales Representatives of Services, Except Advertising, Insurance, Financial Services, and Travel
Pesticide Handlers, Sprayers, and Applicators, Vegetation
Secretaries and Administrative Assistants, Except Legal, Medical, and Executive

n_band.derivation: margin 0.1565 (margins.json[56]: Damodaran Jan 2026: Business & Consumer Services EBITDA/Sales 15.65%); revenue/employee $152,919 (SUSB 2022 receipts/employment at 561730); class <5: 82,695 firms, avg revenue/firm $299,970 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $46,945 -> out of $1-10M band; class 5-9: 16,643 firms, avg revenue/firm $933,160 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $146,040 -> out of $1-10M band; class 10-19: 8,936 firms, avg revenue/firm $1,879,235 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $294,100 -> out of $1-10M band; class 20-99: 5,888 firms, avg revenue/firm $5,038,071 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $788,458 -> out of $1-10M band; class 100-499: 593 firms, avg revenue/firm $22,899,949 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $3,583,842 -> IN $1-10M band; class 500+: 87 firms, avg revenue/firm $206,781,494 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $32,361,304 -> out of $1-10M band; n_band = 593
n_band.margin_source: Damodaran Jan 2026: Business & Consumer Services EBITDA/Sales 15.65%

breakdown_by_role.rationale: Physical outdoor work — mowing, planting, pruning, pesticide application, installation. Current AI does not perform it. Robotic/autonomous mowers are a robotics-capex substitution channel with separate economics and early commercial adoption; per the no-robotics-inflation rule they are excluded from the AI-automatable fraction.
On-site crew leadership is physical/interpersonal. AI scheduling, route planning and job documentation widen span of control only marginally.
Job costing, scheduling, fleet reporting and financial reporting automate substantially inside existing FSM platforms; client relationships, labor recruitment and crew management persist.
AI property measurement/takeoff from aerial imagery and automated quoting compress estimating labor substantially; relationship selling on commercial accounts persists.

Invoicing, scheduling/dispatch entry, payroll for a seasonal hourly workforce, bookkeeping — near-fully automatable with current AI plus existing software; small residual for exception handling and phone traffic.

breakdown_by_role.role: Remainder / long tail (mechanics, drivers, irrigation techs, designers, admin tail)
breakdown_by_role.rationale: Equipment maintenance and driving are physical; landscape design/CAD and the admin tail are AI-exposed. Blended low fraction.

pi_dist.rationale: Commercial maintenance (the dominant revenue mode for band-size firms) is procured by property/facility managers, often nationally, and re-bid annually or tri-annually on competitive tender against near-zero switching costs — cost savings from AI or robotics get competed away at the next bid cycle. BrightView, the only large public pure-play, runs only a 13.2% adjusted EBITDA margin at $2.7B scale (record year, FY2025), evidence that commercial pricing power is chronically thin. Residential recurring and design-build books are owned by the local brand and quoted per job, retaining somewhat more of any cost saving. Blended toward the commercial mode: 0.35.

pi_moat.rationale: Entry barriers are near zero (a truck, a trailer, a crew); no licensure except pesticide applicators; commercial switching costs are low by design of the tender process. Offsets: physical local presence is mandatory (clients cannot bypass the firm with generic AI — grass is cut on site), and route/branch density confers a real unit-cost advantage that a new entrant cannot instantly replicate. Cheap universal AI back-office tools cut minimum efficient scale further and mildly increase fragmentation pressure.

t50_years.derivation: Current material adoption ~20% (see current_adoption_pct: 43% of L&L 2026 Tech Report respondents 'always' or 'usually' use AI tools, but mostly generative chat for content/comms; material use in the automatable functions — AI takeoff/estimating, automated invoicing/dispatch — is well below that; Aspire's 2025 industry report was widely cited as 83% not having adopted AI). Historical analog: field-service management software took roughly 10 years from the ~2010 launch wave (Service Autopilot 2009, LMN, Aspire 2013) to ~50%+ penetration, and 93% now use some digital software. AI features ship inside those already-installed platforms (Aspire, Jobber, SingleOps), so diffusion runs faster than the standalone-software analog: ~20% -> 50% in about 4.5 years.

current_adoption_pct.source: Lawn & Landscape 2026 Technology Report (discounted; see figure_quoted)
current_adoption_pct.figure_quoted: "32% 'always' use AI tools like ChatGPT; 11% 'usually' rely on AI; 30% are occasional users." Material = AI substantially performing an automatable function (AI takeoff/estimating, automated invoicing, dispatch/scheduling automation), not occasional ChatGPT use for content. The 43% always/usually figure is dominated by generative chat for marketing/comms; secondary sources citing Aspire's 2025 Landscape Industry Report report 83% of professionals had NOT adopted AI tools. Declared material adoption: 20%.

historical_analogs.value: Business-management/field-service software: ~10 years from the ~2010 launch wave (Service Autopilot 2009, LMN, Aspire 2013) to majority penetration; 93% of surveyed landscape businesses now use digital software daily. Smartphone/GPS crew tracking diffused faster (~7-8 years).
historical_analogs.source: Aspire landscaping industry statistics (software penetration endpoint); vendor founding dates public record
historical_analogs.figure_quoted: "93% of surveyed businesses use digital software in their daily operations." "51% of landscapers prioritize all-in-one software solutions like Aspire."

owners_60plus_pct.source: No landscaping-specific owner-age distribution found; estimated from national small-business owner demographics adjusted down for a physically demanding trade
owners_60plus_pct.figure_quoted: "More than one-third (37%) of small business owners [are] between 55 and 73 years old" (2024, national all-industry). Landscaping ownership skews slightly younger given physical demands (average landscaper age ~40 per Zippia); estimated 30% of owners aged 60+.

succession_shortage_documented.evidence: Generic small-business statistics (e.g., ~80% of owners lack a documented transition plan) and NALP succession-planning content exist, but no landscaping-specific evidence of a shortage of successors was found; the deep PE bid for these assets argues against a buyer/successor shortage. Not documented.

active_consolidators.source: Trade-press count of named PE-backed landscape platforms with add-ons in the last 24 months: Yellowstone Landscape (Harvest Partners/Neuberger Berman minority Dec 2024), Sperber (New Water Capital), Mariani Premier Group (add-ons MD Nursery, Landscape East & West 2024-25), Juniper Landscaping (Bregal; add-on Landscape Maintenance Professionals), Aspen Grove (Trivest), LandCare, Schill Grounds Management (TruArc, ex-Argonne; add-ons Begonia Brothers, Atlas Outdoor), HeartLand, Monarch Landscape Companies, Landscape Workshop (majority stake sold 2025), LMC Landscape Partners (add-on Shinto Landscaping), The Grounds Guys/Neighborly (Roark)
active_consolidators.figure_quoted: Lawn & Landscape June 2025: 'Majority stake in Landscape Workshop sold; Schill expands'; PrivSource: 'LMC Landscape Partners Acquires Shinto Landscaping'; 'Harvest Partners Acquires Yellowstone Landscape'; Neuberger Berman minority stake in Yellowstone Dec 2024. Count of distinct named PE-backed platforms with add-ons in trailing 24 months: 12.

buy_mult.source: GF Data 2025 full-year median for home-services segment, $10-50M EV (landscape-maintenance subset IQR 5.3-6.6x), as republished in CT Acquisitions Landscaping M&A Multiples Report 2026 (secondary republication — marked as such)
buy_mult.figure_quoted: "GF Data reporting '5.9x adjusted EBITDA' for 2025 full-year transactions" ($10M-$50M enterprise values, home services; landscape subset IQR 5.3x-6.6x). Corroborating: Baird Home Services Q4 2025, 7 disclosed landscape add-ons at blended 6.2x; IBBA Market Pulse Q1 2026 median 4.3x adjusted EBITDA for smaller ($500K-$2M) deals. Figures are adjusted (post-owner-comp) EBITDA, satisfying the normalization rule; sub-$500K SDE multiples were excluded.

exit_mult.source: PitchBook 2025 landscaping platform transactions, as republished in CT Acquisitions Landscaping M&A Multiples Report 2026 (secondary republication — marked as such)
exit_mult.figure_quoted: "Platform Scale ($10M+ Adjusted EBITDA): '7.5x to 10.0x adjusted EBITDA' per PitchBook 2025, with '22 landscaping and lawn care platform transactions' averaging '8.7x adjusted EBITDA' and ranging '7.2x to 10.1x'."

pricing_structure.value: Commercial maintenance: fixed-fee annual/multi-year contracts, competitively re-bid annually or tri-annually via property/facility-manager tender — largest revenue mode for band-size ($20M+ revenue) firms. Residential maintenance: recurring per-visit/monthly fixed fee, locally branded, stickier. Design-build/installation: per-job fixed bid, project-based and cyclical. Snow removal (northern firms): seasonal contract or per-event, weather-volatile. Essentially no hourly/cost-plus billing, but competitive re-tendering of fixed-fee commercial work functions like cost-plus over a bid cycle: cost improvements are competed into price. BrightView (commercial pure-play): 13.2% adjusted EBITDA margin FY2025 — a record — evidencing structurally thin commercial pricing power.
pricing_structure.source: BrightView FY2025 results (8-K exhibit); Lawn & Landscape / Landscape Management trade coverage of commercial re-bid structure
pricing_structure.figure_quoted: "Record adjusted EBITDA of $352.3 million"; "record adjusted EBITDA margin of 13.2%, representing a 150 basis point improvement year-over-year" (BrightView FY2025).

cross_checks.uplift_vs_A_consistent: V_raw of 0.0389715885 implies a maximum EBITDA uplift of ~3.90pp of revenue if all automatable labor cost were captured. Uplift is above the 3pp threshold and A = 2.22 is well below 5, so no inconsistency: modest uplift paired with slow-to-moderate adoption is internally coherent.

terminal_value.justification: Grass is cut and beds are planted by crews physically on site; ~72% of the wage bill (field labor + supervision) is untouched by mature AI, so paid demand fully survives — AI cuts a thin office/sales/estimating wrapper of the operator's costs, it does not replace the operator's product. The decisive issue is capture, not survival: with annual competitive re-bids on commercial maintenance and near-zero entry barriers, savings from AI back-office tools flow to property managers at the next bid cycle (BrightView's 13.2% record margin shows how thin commercial pricing power stays). Robotic mowing is a separate robotics-capex substitution channel with uncertain commercial timeline and was kept strictly out of the AI-labor channel. Demand: DURABLE.

cross_checks.no_proxy_confirmed: Confirmed. V was built solely from the OEWS role mix and per-role task automatability judgments (field labor set to 0.0). A was derived from the Lawn & Landscape 2026 Technology Report adoption figures and the field-service-software diffusion analog. No deal activity, consolidation trend, or PE narrative was used for V or A; PE platform activity informed only CFD (within B) and buy/exit multiples (M).
cross_checks.pricing_model_deflation: Billing mix: fixed-fee commercial maintenance contracts (annual/tri-annual competitive re-bid; dominant for band-size firms), recurring residential fees, per-job design-build bids, seasonal snow contracts. Although nothing is billed cost-plus or hourly, competitive re-tendering of commercial fixed-fee work transmits cost savings to clients within one bid cycle — economically equivalent to cost-plus at annual frequency. This deflation is reflected in pi_dist = 0.35 (gatekeeper procurement + re-bid pass-through), and near-zero entry barriers are reflected in pi_moat = 0.40; C = 1.4 is low accordingly.

top_uncertain_inputs.score_impact: A = 10/t50: t50=3 gives A=3.33, t50=8 gives A=1.25 — S moves roughly from 2.37 to 1.95 (about +/-0.2 on S), the single largest lever.

top_uncertain_inputs.plausible_range: 4.3-6.6 (IBBA 4.3x small deals to GF landscape-subset high 6.6x)
top_uncertain_inputs.score_impact: M = 4*(8.7-buy)/buy: buy=4.3 gives M=4.09, buy=6.6 gives M=1.27 — S ranges roughly 1.96 to 2.55.
OW ranges 0.4286-0.8571; B ranges ~4.67-6.60; S moves by roughly -0.06/+0.09.

reviewer_note: Formula result (S~2.18) matches intuition directionally: this is a B/M-led roll-up story (593 in-band targets, deep PE bid, real multiple arbitrage) with a structurally weak AI story — 61% of the wage bill is field labor scored at 0.0 automatable, so V=1.56 despite a high labor share. Conflicts/limitations: (1) buy_mult and exit_mult come from a secondary republication (ctacquisitions.com) of GF Data/PitchBook/Baird figures; primary reports are paywalled; Principium Group could not be verified as a live source. (2) current_adoption_pct is LOW quality — the L&L 43% always/usually AI figure is generative-chat-dominated while the Aspire-derived 83%-not-adopted figure could not be verified at the primary source (403); the declared 20% material adoption drives t50. (3) owners_60plus_pct is a national small-business estimate, no landscaping-specific age data found; succession-shortage flag left false, so no OW bonus. (4) Mix heterogeneity within 561730 (commercial maintenance vs residential vs design-build vs tree care) is real but 561730 is not a catch-all code; scored on the commercial-maintenance-weighted profile of band-size firms; heterogeneous=false. (5) Dataset n_band=593 rests on a 15.65% Damodaran margin that is generous vs BrightView's 13.2% record commercial margin — used as given per rule 6; if true margins are lower, n_band shifts but the count stays in the hundreds. (6) EBITDA-to-FCF gap from heavy equipment capex means M overstates cash-on-cash arbitrage somewhat; multiples used are adjusted-EBITDA basis, SDE figures excluded.
